revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: 「'''『尾張国風土記』'''（おわりのくにふどき）は、[[奈良時代]]に編纂された[[尾張国]]（現在の[[愛知県]]西部）の[[風土記]]である。全編は現存しておらず、[[逸文]]の形で僅かに伝わるのみである。 == 概要 == [[8世紀]]初頭に編纂された尾張国の風土記である。現存する他の風土記と同様に国内の地名や伝承が書かれていたとみられるが、…」
/* 吾縵郷 */

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:尾張国風土記]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none)
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none)
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Owari no Kuni Fudoki''' (尾張国風土記) was the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The full text does not survive, and only a few fragments ([[:en:Lost literary work|fragments]]) are preserved through later sources.

== Overview ==
The ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' was compiled in the early [[:en:8th century|8th century]]. Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as [[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]], {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|qq=|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.

A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.

== Contents ==
The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.

=== Atsuta Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married [[:en:Miyazu-hime|Miyazu-hime]], an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}

This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.

=== Miyake Temple ===
{{Quotation|
Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.

=== Azura Township ===
{{Quotation|
During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}

This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|qq=|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.

=== Kawashima Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|qq=|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

== Notes ==
{{Notelist}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==

During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}

This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.

=== Kawashima Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:尾張国風土記]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none)
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none)
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Owari no Kuni Fudoki''' (尾張国風土記) was the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The full text does not survive, and only a few fragments ([[:en:Lost literary work|fragments]]) are preserved through later sources.

== Overview ==
The ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' was compiled in the early [[:en:8th century|8th century]]. Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as [[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]], {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.

A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.

== Contents ==
The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.

=== Atsuta Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married [[:en:Miyazu-hime|Miyazu-hime]], an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}

This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.

=== Miyake Temple ===
{{Quotation|
Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.

=== Azura Township ===
{{Quotation|
During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}

This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.

=== Kawashima Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

<!--Owari no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Owari no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Owari no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Owari no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Owari no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Owari no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Owari no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Owari no Kuni Fudoki--> [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]]
{{wikidata link|Q135598023}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:尾張国風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Owari no Kuni Fudoki''' (尾張国風土記) was the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The full text does not survive, and only a few fragments ([[:en:Lost literary work|fragments]]) are preserved through later sources.
== Overview == The ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' was compiled in the early [[:en:8th century|8th century]]. Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as [[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]], {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.
== Contents == The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
=== Atsuta Shrine === {{Quotation| Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married [[:en:Miyazu-hime|Miyazu-hime]], an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}
This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.
=== Miyake Temple === {{Quotation| Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}. |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
=== Azura Township === {{Quotation| During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}
This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.
=== Kawashima Shrine === {{Quotation| Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}} |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

== Contents == The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
=== Atsuta Shrine === {{Quotation| Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married [[:en:Miyazu-hime|Miyazu-hime]], an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}
This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.
=== Miyake Temple === {{Quotation| Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}. |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
=== Azura Township === {{Quotation| During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}
This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.
=== Kawashima Shrine === {{Quotation| Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}} |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}. |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
=== Azura Township === {{Quotation| During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}

The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}} |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

'''Owari no Kuni Fudoki''' (尾張国風土記) was the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The full text does not survive, and only a few fragments ([[:en:Lost literary work|fragments]]) are preserved through later sources.

== Overview ==
The ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' was compiled in the early [[:en:8th century|8th century]]. Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as [[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]], {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.

A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.

== Contents ==
The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.

=== Atsuta Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married [[:en:Miyazu-hime|Miyazu-hime]], an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}

This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.

=== Miyake Temple ===
{{Quotation|
Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.

=== Azura Township ===
{{Quotation|
During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}

This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.

=== Kawashima Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}

This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.

=== Miyake Temple ===
{{Quotation|
Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.

=== Azura Township ===
{{Quotation|
During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}

This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.

=== Kawashima Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

The full text does not survive, and only a few fragments ([[:en:Lost literary work|fragments]]) are preserved through later sources.
== Overview == The ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' was compiled in the early [[:en:8th century|8th century]]. Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as [[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]], {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.

Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}
This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.

During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}. |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
=== Azura Township === {{Quotation| During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}
This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.

'''Owari no Kuni Fudoki''' (尾張国風土記) was the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The full text does not survive, and only a few fragments ([[:en:Lost literary work|fragments]]) are preserved through later sources.

== Overview ==
The ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' was compiled in the early [[:en:8th century|8th century]]. Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as [[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]], {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.

A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.

== Contents ==
The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.

=== Atsuta Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married [[:en:Miyazu-hime|Miyazu-hime]], an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}

This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.

=== Miyake Temple ===
{{Quotation|
Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.

=== Azura Township ===
{{Quotation|
During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}

This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.

=== Kawashima Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.

{{Quotation|
During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}

This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.

=== Kawashima Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

'''Owari no Kuni Fudoki''' (尾張国風土記) was the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The full text does not survive, and only a few fragments ([[:en:Lost literary work|fragments]]) are preserved through later sources.

== Overview ==
The ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' was compiled in the early [[:en:8th century|8th century]]. Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as [[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]], {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.

A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.

== Contents ==
The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.

=== Atsuta Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married [[:en:Miyazu-hime|Miyazu-hime]], an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}

This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of [[:en:Atsuta Shrine|Atsuta Shrine]], detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.

=== Miyake Temple ===
{{Quotation|
Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.

=== Azura Township ===
{{Quotation|
During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}
|''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}

This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in [[:en:Niwa District|Niwa District]], linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.

=== Kawashima Shrine ===
{{Quotation|
Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}
|''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}

This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:尾張国風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:尾張国風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:尾張国風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:24, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Owari no Kuni Fudoki''' (尾張国風土記) was the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}}, compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}. The full text does not survive, and only a few fragments ({{ill|Lost literary work|en|Lost literary work|WD=Q1585442|lt=fragments}}) are preserved through later sources.
== Overview == The ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' was compiled in the early {{ill|8th century|en|8th century|WD=Q8086|lt=8th century}}. Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihongi}}, {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.
== Contents == The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
=== Atsuta Shrine === {{Quotation| Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married {{ill|Miyazu-hime|en|Miyazu-hime|WD=Q11455216|lt=Miyazu-hime}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}
This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|en|Atsuta Shrine|WD=Q482065|lt=Atsuta Shrine}}, detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.
=== Miyake Temple === {{Quotation| Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}. |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
=== Azura Township === {{Quotation| During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}
This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in {{ill|Niwa District|en|Niwa District|WD=Q1347719|lt=Niwa District}}, linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.
=== Kawashima Shrine === {{Quotation| Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}} |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=栗田寛|title=古風土記逸文|publisher=大岡山書店|year=1927|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1210472/1/44|ref=harv}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Owari no Kuni Fudoki''' (尾張国風土記) was the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}}, compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}. The full text does not survive, and only a few fragments ({{ill|Lost literary work|en|Lost literary work|WD=Q1585442|lt=fragments}}) are preserved through later sources.
== Overview == The ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' was compiled in the early {{ill|8th century|en|8th century|WD=Q8086|lt=8th century}}. Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihongi}}, {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.
== Contents == The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
=== Atsuta Shrine === {{Quotation| Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married {{ill|Miyazu-hime|en|Miyazu-hime|WD=Q11455216|lt=Miyazu-hime}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}
This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|en|Atsuta Shrine|WD=Q482065|lt=Atsuta Shrine}}, detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.
=== Miyake Temple === {{Quotation| Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}. |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
=== Azura Township === {{Quotation| During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}
This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in {{ill|Niwa District|en|Niwa District|WD=Q1347719|lt=Niwa District}}, linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.
=== Kawashima Shrine === {{Quotation| Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}} |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.
== Notes == {{Notelist}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|author=栗田寛|title=古風土記逸文|publisher=大岡山書店|year=1927|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1210472/1/44|ref=harv}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|尾張国風土記|version=102985161|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:尾張国風土記]]

Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihongi|1=Shaku Nihongi}}, {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.
== Contents == The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
=== Atsuta Shrine === {{Quotation| Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married {{ill|Miyazu-hime|en|Miyazu-hime|WD=Q11455216|lt=Miyazu-hime|1=Miyazu-hime}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}
This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|en|Atsuta Shrine|WD=Q482065|lt=Atsuta Shrine|1=Atsuta Shrine}}, detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.
=== Miyake Temple === {{Quotation| Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}. |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
=== Azura Township === {{Quotation| During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}
This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in {{ill|Niwa District|en|Niwa District|WD=Q1347719|lt=Niwa District|1=Niwa District}}, linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.
=== Kawashima Shrine === {{Quotation| Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}} |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:尾張国風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:尾張国風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:尾張国風土記]]
'''Owari no Kuni Fudoki''' (尾張国風土記) was the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. The full text does not survive, and only a few fragments ({{ill|Lost literary work|en|Lost literary work|WD=Q1585442|lt=fragments|1=Lost literary work}}) are preserved through later sources.
== Overview == The ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' was compiled in the early {{ill|8th century|en|8th century|WD=Q8086|lt=8th century|1=8th century}}. Like other extant Fudoki, it likely recorded local place names and traditions. However, much of the text was lost by the Edo period. The fragments now available are citations in later works such as {{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihongi|1=Shaku Nihongi}}, {{ill|Chinbukuro|ja|塵袋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinbukuro|1=Chiribukuro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17213633}}, and {{ill|Man'yōshū Commentary|ja|万葉集註釈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Man'yōshū Commentary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
A work titled ''Owari Fudoki'' is held by the {{ill|Aichi Prefectural Library|ja|愛知県立図書館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aichi Prefectural Library|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 愛知県図書館|1=Aichi Prefectural Library|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11256818}}, but it is part of the early modern forgery ''Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki'' and unrelated to the original ancient compilation<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://websv.aichi-pref-library.jp/wahon/detail/95.html|title=【地誌】尾張国風土記|accessdate=2024-12-01|publisher=Aichi Prefectural Library}}</ref>.
== Contents == The surviving fragments attributed to the ''Fudoki of Owari Province'' are summarized below{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|pp=47-58}}.
=== Atsuta Shrine === {{Quotation| Atsuta Shrine: In ancient times, during the return of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} from his journey to the eastern provinces, he married {{ill|Miyazu-hime|en|Miyazu-hime|WD=Q11455216|lt=Miyazu-hime|1=Miyazu-hime}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張連|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Staying at her home, Yamato Takeru hung his sword on a mulberry tree near the restroom. When he went to retrieve it, the sword glowed with divine radiance, leading him to declare it sacred. He instructed Miyazu-hime to enshrine the sword as his own shadow and reflection, thus establishing Atsuta Shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=47}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 7}}
This fragment is cited in ''Shaku Nihongi'' and describes the origins of {{ill|Atsuta Shrine|en|Atsuta Shrine|WD=Q482065|lt=Atsuta Shrine|1=Atsuta Shrine}}, detailing the enshrinement of the {{ill|Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi no Tsurugi|lt=Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja|草薙剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天叢雲剣}}. It parallels accounts in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' and ''{{ill|Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja|尾張国熱田太神宮縁起|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari no Kuni Atsuta Daijingū Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DB/0049/DB00490R027.pdf|title=『尾張国熱田太神宮縁起』を読む-『古語拾遺』との比較から-|accessdate=2024-12-01|author=Taniguchi Jun|editor=Bukkyo University Graduate School of Literature|format=PDF}}</ref>.
=== Miyake Temple === {{Quotation| Owari Province, Aichi District, Fukuyuki Temple{{Efn|Also called Fukuyoshi Temple.}} (commonly known as Miyake Temple), located 9 ''ri'' and 14 steps south of the district seat in the Hinokuma Village of Nikabe Township. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, in the first year of Jingkei (724), the temple was built by Miyake no Muraji Masa, a senior official (''Jushichi-i''){{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}. |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment describes Fukuyuki Temple, also known as Miyake Temple, in Aichi District. It states that the temple was established in 724 by a local official, Miyake no Muraji Masa{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}}.
=== Azura Township === {{Quotation| During the reign of Emperor Suinin at the Makimuku no Mizugaki Palace, Prince Homutsuwake reached the age of seven but had not yet spoken. The Empress dreamed of a god identifying itself as Amanomikatsuhime, demanding worship. The god claimed that by assigning someone to serve them, the prince would gain speech and long life. The Emperor ordered a divination, and Takao no Kimi was sent to locate the deity. He made a garland (''kazura'') from sacred tree branches, saying, "Where this garland falls, the god must reside." It fell in this place, and a shrine was built. The name Azura comes from this origin.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=51}} |''Shaku Nihongi,'' Volume 10}}
This passage explains the etymology of "Azura Township" in {{ill|Niwa District|en|Niwa District|WD=Q1347719|lt=Niwa District|1=Niwa District}}, linked to the founding of the {{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11657682}}.
=== Kawashima Shrine === {{Quotation| Kawashima Shrine: Located in Kawashima Village of Kawanuma Township, Hakuri District. During the reign of Emperor Shōmu, it was reported by Omikumi no Oshihito that the deity appeared as a white deer. The Emperor issued a decree to enshrine the deity as a heavenly shrine.{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}} |''Man'yōshū Commentary,'' Volume 1}}
This fragment concerns {{ill|Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|ja|川島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawashima Shrine (Kōnan City)|1=Kawashima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16766691}} in Hakuri District. It recounts the shrine's establishment during the reign of Emperor Shōmu after a divine manifestation{{Sfn|Kurita Kan|1927|p=50}}.
